UKMTO Route Risk Monitor

Different Route → Different Proximity to Incidents → Different Risk Index

Metrics

Risk Index
—
Σ(severity × recency × proximity)
Incidents (filtered)
—

Incidents

Last update: —
Status: —
View project on GitHub
Risk Index definition
Risk Index (0–100)

The Risk Index estimates the relative operational risk for vessels travelling along the selected route. It aggregates nearby maritime security incidents and weights them by severity, recency, and proximity to the route.

Step 1 — Raw Risk
Each incident contributes a score based on three components:

RawRisk = Σ (severity × recency × proximity)

severity
The severity score is derived from the incident description using a deterministic keyword and context scoring rule. Terms associated with weapons, attacks, damage, or casualties increase the score, while language indicating uncertainty or lack of harm (e.g., “no injuries”, “suspected”, “attempted”) reduces it.

Interpretation of the scale:
• Suspicious activity or approach → low severity
• Boarding attempts or hostile interaction → moderate severity
• Confirmed attacks involving weapons, explosions, or casualties → high severity

The score is capped at 20 to prevent a single incident from dominating the overall risk calculation. recency
Recent incidents carry more weight than older ones using exponential decay:

recency = 0.5^(age_days / half_life_days)

The parameter half_life_days controls how quickly incidents lose influence. For example, if half-life = 7 days, an incident contributes half as much after one week.

proximity
Incidents closer to the shipping route are more relevant:

proximity = exp(−distance_to_lane_km / radius_km)

The parameter radius_km determines the spatial decay. Incidents directly on the route receive full weight, while distant events contribute less.

Step 2 — Convert Raw Risk to Index
RawRisk is converted into a bounded index between 0 and 100:

RiskIndex = 100 × (1 − exp(−RawRisk / 15))

This transformation prevents the score from increasing indefinitely when many incidents occur. Instead, the index saturates as risk accumulates, allowing scores to remain comparable across different routes and time periods.

The constant 15 controls the scaling of this saturation curve. Lower values make the index rise quickly; higher values make it increase more gradually.
